observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
(a) require the Local Development Council to (i) immediately submit the Supplemental Annual Investment Program (AIP) related to the additional PPAs appropriated under the Supplemental Budget No. 06 for CY 2023; and (ii) ensure that the 20% DF is utilized to finance the LGU’s priority development projects consistent with its duly approved Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP) and updated AIP; (b) instruct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to (i) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed development projects through adequate planning and close monitoring; and (ii) verify the actual status of completion of projects at year-end to provide reasonably accurate information to the concerned offices/departments; (c) submit written justification on the continuation of contracts with and non-imposition of liquidated damages to the contractors relative to the projects that incurred negative slippages of more than 15%; (d) direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) require the contractors to comply with the prescribed posting of information and publicity on PPAs and inform the AT within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the project site; and (ii) through the conduct of regular inspection, ensure that the information posted in the project signboards are complete, accurate and updated as required by COA Circular No. 2013-004; (e) instruct the MBO to (i) coordinate with the concerned offices/departments and gather information relevant to the preparation of the 20% DF Utilization Report to ensure accurate and transparent reporting; (ii) reflect the actual cost incurred of each PPA by considering the percentage of completion at report date prepared and verified by the MEO; and (iii) ensure that all expenditures charged against the 20% DF are valid expenditures in accordance with Section 3.2.5 of the JMC, and refrain from charging expenditures that are administrative in nature; and (f) direct the MA, in coordination with the BAC, to (i) immediately transmit the unsubmitted DVs related to the Fund, together with complete supporting documents, to facilitate prompt verification and timely communication to Management of any audit deficiencies; and (ii) draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the errors previously discussed, if warranted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant (MA) to (i) trace and analyze the composition of the
CIP account and make the necessary adjustments to correct the account
balance, particularly regarding the (1) misclassified transaction
amounting to ₱2,968,926.00, and (2) costs of completed projects totaling
₱11,559,935.41 that remain in the account, if warranted; (ii) ensure that
the construction cost of all public infrastructure projects are properly
accrued to the appropriate PPE accounts based on the percentage of
completion as reported by the MEO, including costs of two projects
completed in CY 2024 totaling ₱3,770,905.03 and the related accumulated
depreciation from the date of completion amounting to ₱81,769.74; and
(iii) coordinate closely with the MEO to monitor the progress of
infrastructure projects, especially during the preparation of year-end
financial reports;
b) Municipal Engineer to furnish the MAO, and other concerned offices with
a copy of the Report on Status of Implementation of all Infrastructure
Projects to facilitate accurate and timely accounting of CIP, particularly
at year-end;
c) MA and GSO Head to (i) prepare and maintain PPELCs and PCs,
respectively, for each type of PPE; (ii) exert collaborative effort in
strategizing the conduct of complete physical inventory and other activities
relative to the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2020-006; and (iii) investigate and reconcile the noted
discrepancies between the accounting records and the result of physical
inventory; and
d) GSO Head to (i) ensure the registration of all motor vehicles with the LTO
to prevent the incurrence of unnecessary penalties and fines; and (ii)
secure insurance coverage of all insurable properties with the GSIS to
mitigate risks in the event of damage to, or loss of, properties due to fire,
earthquake, storm, or other catastrophes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
a) MA to (i) ensure that all necessary adjustments are made at year-end to
accurately report Cash – Local Treasury account balances per fund; and
(ii) accurately record all collections in the appropriate accounting period
corresponding to the date of receipt; and
b) Municipal Treasurer (MT) to (i) ensure that all collections are deposited to
the appropriate fund to prevent negative balances in the Cash – Local
Treasury account per fund; and (ii) promptly deposit all collections intact,
either on the day of receipt or no later than the next banking day, thereby
properly safeguarding government funds from risks of theft, loss,
misapplication, and/or misappropriation.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) MA to (i) ensure compliance with Section 20, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for
LGUs in the proper recognition and setting up of RPT/SET Receivables
and ensure the correct report was used as the basis; and (ii) recognize fines
and penalties in the year it was collected in accordance with Section 23,
Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs;
b) MT to review and re-calculate the Certified List of Delinquent Real
Property Taxpayers so that the accurate amount will be recognized as
RPT/SET Receivable;
c) MA to maintain SLs of RPT/SET Receivable and provide supporting
documents/computations for adjustments made;
d) Municipal Assessor, MT, and MA to reconcile all RPT-related reports,
such as the Assessment Roll, Certified List of Delinquent Real Property
Taxpayers, and the Abstract of RPTs. This is to identify and address any
discrepancies in a timely manner and to uphold the accuracy, consistency,
and reliability of RPT-related records and reports;
e) Municipal Assessor to ensure that all FAAS are properly accomplished,
and duly signed. Additionally, coordinate with the PAO to secure copies of
all FAAS for properties assessed at the PAO level. A well-organized,
complete, and readily available filing system must be maintained to
support accurate assessments and to facilitate future audits; and
f) MA (i) conduct a comprehensive assessment of the collectability of its RPT
receivables, considering key factors such as the aging of accounts,
historical collection rates/trends, and the prevailing economic conditions
of the locality; (ii) establish an appropriate allowance for impairment to
fairly present the estimated uncollectible portion of RPT receivables in
the financial statements; (iii) implement policies and procedures to regularly
review and adjust the impairment allowance as necessary; and (iv) prepare
and record the necessary adjusting journal entries to ensure accuracy and
reliability of financial records.

We recommended that Management ensure strict compliance with the existing
regulations governing the granting, utilization, and liquidation of CAs issued to
SDOs. Specifically, Management should (i) enforce the full liquidation of all CAs at
year-end; (ii) implement close monitoring of liquidation activities to ensure timely
settlement within the prescribed period; and (iii) strictly prohibit the granting of
additional CAs to any SDO with outstanding or unliquidated balances from prior
advances.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) HRMO Head to (i) prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances
in the employee leave cards; (ii) revisit its process of maintaining and
reporting employee leave records, which should include updating and
encoding of employee leave cards regularly, generating quarterly reports
for timely submission to the MAO and other concerned offices, and other
internal control measures to provide reliable information; and (iii) secure
the certified copy of employee leave cards, SALN, and letter of resignation,
if applicable, when processing claims of separated employees; and
b) MA to (i) draw a JEV to record the additional provision for earned leave
credits in the books of accounts of the ensuing year as recommended below:
Account Name Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustment ₱832,495.11
Leave Benefits Payable ₱832,495.11
(ii) recognize subsequent provisions at least quarterly; (iii) ensure that all
unpaid obligations pertaining to earned leave credits of employees are
recorded; (iv) be guided with the suggested journal entries in accounting
for transactions related to leave benefits as illustrated in Annex C.1.3 of
COA Circular No. 2024-009; (v) reclassify the non-current portion of the
Leave Benefits Payable account for proper presentation in the Statement
of Financial Position; (vi) prepare and certify the computation of TLB for
separated employees to ensure accuracy and compliance; and (vii) ensure
the claims are supported with adequate documentation as required under
COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MAO to take the
following actions (i) reconcile the discrepancies noted in the fund transfer with the
concerned government agencies; and (ii) in coordination with the Implementing Unit
like the DSWD, CHED, and DOH, transmit immediately the LR for timely recording
in the SA’s books in compliance with the MOA, guidelines and the above-mentioned
MCs of each NGA.

We recommended that the LCE direct the concerned officials to exert extra
effort in locating and submitting the copies of contracts, DVs, and complete
supporting documents to facilitate the settlement of the remaining balance of audit
suspensions totaling ₱4,206,185.82.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MEO, in
coordination with other concerned departments to (a) submit the lacking documents
mentioned in the preceding table; and (b) undertake comprehensive planning and
coordination with relevant government agencies, such as the DPWH, to prevent
delays in project implementation which ultimately hinder the timely delivery of
benefits intended to local constituents.

We recommended that Management:
a) instruct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator to (i) ensure the effective and efficient
implementation of the proposed development projects through adequate
planning and close monitoring; and (ii) verify the actual status of
completion of projects at year-end to provide reasonably accurate
information to the concerned offices/departments;
b) submit written justification on (i) the inconsistencies noted on the
implementation of the project entitled “Improvement of Subic Park,
Barangay Wawandue;” and (ii) the continuation of contracts with, and
non-imposition of liquidated damages to the contractors relative for the
projects that incurred negative slippages of more than 15%, as presented
in the foregoing discussions;
c) direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) require the contractors to comply with
the prescribed posting of information and publicity on PPAs and inform
the AT within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the
start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboards and/or public
notices are already posted at the project site; and (ii) through the conduct
of regular inspection, ensure that the information posted in the project
signboards are complete, accurate and updated as required by COA
Circular No. 2013-004;
d) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to (i) coordinate with the concerned
offices/departments and gather information relevant to the preparation of
the 20% DF Utilization Report to ensure accurate and transparent
reporting; (ii) reflect the actual cost incurred of each PPA by considering
the percentage of completion at report date prepared and verified by the
MEO; and (iii) ensure that all expenditures charged against the 20% DF
are valid expenditures in accordance with Section 3.2.5 of the JMC, and
refrain from charging expenditures that are administrative in nature; and
e) direct the MA, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC), to (i) immediately transmit the unsubmitted DVs related to the
Fund, together with complete supporting documents, to facilitate prompt
verification and timely communication to Management of any audit
deficiencies; and (ii) draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
errors previously discussed, if warranted.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) HRMO and the MAO to conduct a thorough review and validation of all
teaching assignments involving government personnel serving as part-time
instructors to ensure full compliance with CHED Memorandum Order No.
017, s. 2017, particularly the nine-unit teaching load limit;
b) MAO to likewise verify the legitimacy of disbursements made to
individuals with excessive teaching assignments and initiate appropriate
adjustments or recoveries, if necessary; and
c) HRMO, in coordination with KNS College President, to regularly monitor
the teaching loads of all faculty members to ensure fair workload
distribution, prevent conflicts with official government duties, and
promote the effective utilization of personnel.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) MDRRMC to (i) formulate and implement a comprehensive LDRRMFIP
prioritizing those PPAs that are feasible and responsive to the real needs
of the constituents; (ii) immediately submit the approved LDRRMFIP for
CY 2024 after incorporating the necessary revisions thereof; and (iii)
ensure that any changes to the plan and budget of the LDRRMF are
supported by an approved and updated LDRRMFIP to substantiate the
propriety of disbursements charged thereto;
b) MDRRMC and Municipal Budget Officer to (i) submit a written
explanation together with relevant supporting documents, to justify the
improper charging of expenditures totaling ₱11,378,475.00 against the
LDRRMF, which appear to be administrative in nature; and (ii) refrain
from using the LDRRMF for expenditures which are not directly related
to disaster preparedness, mitigation, response and rescue operations; and
c) MA to (i) coordinate with the BAC, and the MDRRMC, to immediately
secure and submit to the AT the DVs, contracts, and all relevant
supporting documents pertaining to the three disbursements totaling
₱13,516,400.00 related to the procurement of rice, rescue tools, and
accessories charged against the LDRRMF; and (ii) strictly comply with the
submission of contracts or purchase orders (POs) within five working days
from the execution thereof in accordance with Sections 3.1 and 3.2 of COA
Circular 2009-001 for all subsequent procurements.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the GFPS to ensure the submission of the GAD AR with complete
supporting documents required under Section 5.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-
DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
b) issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office
that conducted the review of the LGU GPB, to ensure the dissemination
and implementation of the GPB; and
c) issue an updated policy directive to reorganize and strengthen the GFPS,
the GFPS TWG and the M&E Team, ensuring their composition are fully
aligned with the requirements set forth in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC
No. 2013-01.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of actions:
(a) MT and MA to submit the certified statement as required under Section
5.5 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure accurate SEF
income projections;
(b) LSB to submit the Statement of Funding Source for the Supplemental
Budget and the copy of the Ordinance for the Augmentation and ensure
that Supplemental Budget and Augmentation are supported with the
required reports before making any changes in the SEF Budget;
(c) through the MT, refrain from making payments through CAs for the
procurement of goods and draw checks directly in favor of the suppliers;
(d) BAC to (i) adopt public bidding as the primary mode of procurement and
proceed to alternative methods only when the required conditions are met;
(ii) stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions and POs/contracts
and adhere to the rules and regulations in the procurement of capital
assets, goods, and services as provided under the Updated 2016 RIRR of
R.A. No. 9184; and (iii) make sure that only those designated by the HoPE
with adequate procurement proficiency are included as BAC Members in
the procurement of goods and services;
(e) School BAC Members to submit a written justification on the procurement
of instructional materials totaling ₱503,358.45 with questionable
signatures on its supporting documents;
(f) LSB, through the General Services Officer/Property Custodian,
immediately conduct a thorough reconciliation of the cellphone
distribution, present the undistributed and unused cellphones, and submit
the supporting documentation for the distribution of all cellphones to the
AT; otherwise refund the total amount of ₱208,440.00 or equivalent to 27
Cellphones;
(g) LSB to submit written justification/legal basis and supporting documents,
such as accepted research proposals and certification from the DepEd
Schools Division Office – Zambales that no communication allowance was
granted to DepEd – District of Subic Personnel, for the

payment/expenses totaling ₱27,827.08 and submit the nine DVs totaling
₱11,014,005.45;

ensure completeness of recording of financial transactions and maintain

Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017, to enhance monitoring,
transparency, and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the SEF,
for the betterment of the education sector.

a) ensure that all financial assistance programs or disbursements are
supported by either a Sangguniang Resolution or formally approved
internal guidelines; and

esspecially for assistance involving varying amounts to be incorporated in
the authorizing resolution or internal guidelines, and applied consistently
to all beneficiaries.

faithfully complying with the withholding and full remittance of the taxes withheld
within the prescribed period; thus, benefiting the government of resources to fund
national and local projects.

the books of accounts the contributions/loans withheld and remitted in a particular
month under the Due to GSIS account, in conformity with the accrual basis of
accounting, to ensure a fair presentation of the financial statements; and (b) perform
the necessary reconciliation between their records and those of the GSIS to trace the
discrepancies noted, for proper disposition.

personnel assigned in the computation of remittance and the recording of premium
contribution to perform regular reconciliation of their records to trace the
discrepancies noted, for proper disposition and ensure the full remittance of all
withheld premiums and loan amortizations from its employees in accordance with
R.A. No. 9679.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure the proper withholding
and full remittance of PhilHealth contributions pursuant to Section 10 of R.A. No.
11223 and PhilHealth Circular No. 0001 series of 2014.

supporting documents to facilitate the settlement of the remaining balance of audit
suspensions totaling ₱4,206,185.82.

observations.recommendation: 1.5	We recommended that Management (a) direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (i) adopt public bidding as the primary mode of procurement; and (ii) adhere strictly to the rules and regulations of R.A. No. 9184, especially in implementing Negotiated Procurement under SVP; (b) refrain from making payments through cash advance and issue checks directly to suppliers; and (c) instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to: (i) coordinate with the BAC for the completion and submission of the documentary requirements and ensure that only DVs with complete documentations are processed and presented for payment; and (ii) submit the DVs for COVID-19 related transactions with complete supporting documents for prompt verification and timely communication of any noted deficiencies.

2.9	We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-MHO and staff, in coordination with the Human Resources Management Officer (HRMO) and OIC-Municipal Accountant, to submit (i) the List of Eligible Frontliners duly approved by the Local Health Board; (ii) DTRs; (iii) duties and responsibilities with Accomplishment Reports of regular, casuals and JO personnel; and (iv) Appointments/Plantilla for casuals, JOs and BHWs for us to evaluate the validity and propriety of the grant and disbursement of the AHDP.

3.4	We recommended that Management instructs the OIC-Municipal Accountant to file a request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers with COA after the conditions and requirements pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 have been fully satisfied; and trace the records/documents relative to the unreturned balance of all completed projects and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for the return of unused funds to the SAs or to the National Treasury, as the case may be.

4.5	We recommended that Management:
instruct the Municipal Engineer/Implementing Unit (i) submit a written justification for the delays on the seven projects; (ii) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed development projects through adequate planning and close monitoring; and (iii) impose liquidated damages to the contractor/s in default or recommend the termination of contracts for negative slippage exceeding 15%, if warranted;
require the BAC Members and Technical Working Group (TWG) to ensure that prospective bidders submit complete and correct eligibility requirements before they are marked as “passed” and declared as Lowest Calculated and Responsive Bid (LCRB) during post-qualification; and
advise the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) in coordination with the BAC members, submit the foregoing lacking documents including the  relative contracts and other documents forming part thereof, within five calendar days after the award to substantiate the disbursements made; (ii) ensure that all payments are supported with complete documentation; and (iii) submit the DVs pertaining to the Fund together with complete supporting documents for prompt verification of the transactions and timely communication to Management of audit deficiencies noted.

5.4	We recommended and Management agreed to direct the:
LDRRMC/MDRRMO to coordinate with other departments in properly monitoring, evaluating, and utilization of the Fund and strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund and the desired benefits; and
OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) review the TL – DRRMF account and make the necessary adjustments; (ii) submit the monthly Utilization Report within the reglementary period; and (iii) properly monitor expenditures charged against the Fund to avoid inconsistencies with other LGU reports.

6.3	We recommended that Management require the:
OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) carefully review the JEVs prepared by accounting staff prior to posting to the books of accounts to minimize errors and immediately correct erroneous accounting entries, particularly the proper classification of items of PPE; (ii) recognize expenditures for asphalting of roads as Repairs and Maintenance Expense instead of capitalizing the cost to Road Networks account in conformity with COA Circular No. 2015-008 (iii) maintain CIPLC and coordinate closely with the CEO to monitor project status to facilitate proper accounting for CIP; and (iv) draw JEVs to record correction on the (1) misclassified properties and relative accumulated depreciation; (2) erroneous capitalization for asphalting of roads; (3) non-adjustment of CIP for completed projects; and (4) unrecorded depreciation for completed projects lodged in the CIP account; as recommended; and
General Services Officer to provide adequate insurance on all its insurable properties to safeguard the same from possible loss due to unforeseen events.

7.4	We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to carefully review the JEVs prepared by the accounting staff prior to posting to the books of accounts to minimize errors and immediately correct erroneous accounting entries; (ii) draw JEVs to record adjustments on misclassified transactions and unrecorded accounts under the Advances to Officer and Employees account, if warranted; (iii) require the concerned accountable officers to liquidate/submit liquidation reports as soon as the purpose of the cash advances have been accomplished; and (iv) strictly prohibit the grant of new or additional cash advances whenever previous ones have not yet been fully settled;
direct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) adhere to the existing rules and regulations regarding disbursements wherein procurement of goods and services shall be made by check directly in favor of the suppliers/creditors; and (ii) limit the cash advances granted to elected officials only to their official traveling expenses as required by the existing rules and regulations on cash advances; and
compel the concerned accountable officers to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances upon demand by the Auditor otherwise, the withholding of salaries shall be imposed on erring accountable officers pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-004.

8.10	We recommended and Management agreed to (a) instruct the Municipal Administrator to relinquish the authority/responsibility to submit the monthly accounts and reports to the Accounting Office under the supervision of the OIC-Municipal Accountant; (b) transfer/reassign the personnel in charge of the so-called “Accounting Office Extension” under the Mayor’s Office to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to augment the workforce thereat; and (c) direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly to the timeframes on the submission of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, otherwise, withhold the salaries of officials/employees who continually neglected to abide by the rules and regulations of Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445.

10.4	We recommended that Management:
direct the MPDC to regularly coordinate with the DENR Officials while updating/revising their 10-Year SWMP to immediately incorporate any changes or additional requirements for the approval of the Municipality’s 10-Year SWMP;
instruct the Municipal Budget and Municipal Engineer to set aside funds and facilitate the construction of MRF; and
intensify the information campaign on the “No Segregation, No Collection” policy and encourage the active participation of the Barangay Officials through continuously educating their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes.

11.4	We recommended that Management (a) issue an EO to reorganize and strengthen the GFPS Executive Committee, the GFPS TWG and the M&E Team in accordance with the required composition of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; (b) utilize the sex-disaggregated data obtained from the existing CBMS as the baseline in the establishment of GAD Database to catalyze performance-based gender-responsive planning on the basis of a reliable information system; and (c) exert additional efforts in the implementation of all planned PPAs.

12.9	We recommended and Management agreed to advise the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) adhere faithfully to RMC No. 23-2012 on the prompt remittance of taxes to avoid the incurrence of interests and surcharges; (ii) adopt systematic procedures in creating subsidiary records in order to report and remit taxes withheld accurately; and (iii) reconcile the SL balances with the GL controlling account for Due to BIR every end of the accounting period.

13.7	We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 8291 and its RIRR on the withholding of premium contributions and loan amortizations and religiously observe the prescribed deadlines on the remittance of the amounts due to the GSIS.

14.7	We recommended and Management agreed to advise the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to remit promptly the amount of premiums and loan amortizations withheld from its employees in accordance with the payment schedule prescribed under R.A. No. 9679.

15.8	We commended the efforts of the Accounting and Treasury Departments and recommend to continue to be compliant with the regulations on the withholding and remittance of monthly PhilHealth contributions.
16.7	We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the audit suspensions as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the persons liable to settle immediately the audit disallowances totaling ₱421,889.94 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; otherwise, as the agency head, initiate legal/administrative sanctions for their continued failure to make settlement.
observations.reason: Partial settlement was made during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of all the properties of the Municipal Government on an annual basis and reconcile the same with the related books of accounts and submit to the Auditor’s Office the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year; and (ii) facilitate the disposal of unserviceable items to prevent  further deterioration and loss of commercial value; and (b) require the maintenance  and keeping of updated Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards including real properties by the Accounting Office, and the Property Cards by the GSO, to facilitate the monitoring and reconciliation process between these offices; and (c) advice the Municipal Accountant to (i) exert effort to locate the pertinent documents that would show the details of the unaccounted balances and to take necessary action to establish the accuracy of the PPE balances; or (ii) consider requesting authority from COA to write off unidentified PPEs or those unsupported beginning balances of PPE accounts following the guidelines under COA Circular No. 93-404.

We recommended and the management agreed to (a) apply effective cash programming to facilitate the proper handling of cash such that it is available whenever needed for payment of obligations and at the same time, optimize its utilization; (b) adopt austerity measures on disbursements to improve the cash position of the Municipality; (c) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (i) coordinate closely in monitoring the cash position of the Municipality to ensure that all disbursements are within the prescribed limitations to avoid incurrence of  cash overdraft and accumulation of unpaid obligations as provided under Section 337 of R.A. No. 7160; and (ii) set aside the IRA intended for 20% Development Fund to be used solely for the purposes it was appropriated and the unused balance of the 5% LDRMMF for transfer to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the management instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) see to it that goods are actually delivered based on duly approved Acceptance and Inspection Reports before recording in the books as inventories; (ii) course regular purchases thru the inventory account; (iii) adopt  the perpetual inventory method in recording purchases of supplies and materials; (iv) record the consumption of supplies and materials in the period these were actually issued on the basis of approved RIS; (v) maintain supplies ledger cards to record all purchases and issuances of supplies and materials; and (vi) see to it that the supplies are properly recorded in the appropriate expenditure accounts immediately upon receipt of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI).

We recommended that the management direct the Municipal Accountant  to (a) submit the CIF disbursement vouchers (DVs)  to the Audit Team within seven days from the release of the check for verification; (b) process additional cash advance only after the liquidation of the previous one; (c) provide certification on the DVs that the previous cash advance for the same purpose was already liquidated and proper accounting thereof has been made; (e) record the liquidation of the CY 2016 cash advances in the books of accounts only upon receipt of the transmittal letter stamped received by the COA-Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit; and (f) henceforth, strictly adhere to the guidelines under COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 on the granting, recording and reporting of confidential and/or intelligence funds for CY 2016 and the years thereafter.

We recommended that the ICO – Municipal Treasurer notify immediately the payees, either in written form or through other means, of the availability of their checks at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to prevent them from becoming stale.  Likewise, instruct the personnel assigned in the preparation of checks to be extra careful in typing the dates, names of payees, amounts in words, and amounts in figures to avoid cancellation of checks due to minor errors pursuant to aforementioned PCHC Memorandum so as to avoid extra cost in terms of replacement of checks.
We recommended that the management require the GSO to provide adequate insurance to all its insurable properties to safeguard government properties from loss due to unforeseen events.

We recommended that the management ensure that (a) payroll deductions be limited following the order of preference provided in the GAA and see to it that an employee’s net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay, which for CY 2016 is ₱3,500.00, as provided under Section 52 of R.A. No. 10717; (b) the paying capacity of each employee-borrower above the minimum mandated take home pay be considered before the approval of loan applications; (c) cease from entering into an agreement which assigns to the Municipality the function of a withholding agent for loan deductions of employees in favor of institutions not authorized under the provisions of the applicable GAA; and (d) hold the approving  officials of payrolls liable for allowing the net take home pay of the employees at lesser amounts than what is prescribed by law.

We recommended that the management: (a) in the case of the leagues of elective officials, cause the immediate submission of disbursement vouchers, liquidation reports and other pertinent documents in accordance with the prevailing practice of any government institution as a requirement in the release of funds and settlement of accounts in order for the Audit Team to render appropriate audit action thereof; and (b) issue a directive informing all concerned leagues of appointive officials for the discontinuance of payment of annual dues or any contributions to their credit and avoidance of charging personal expenses like meals, transportation, rental of function area/amenities and other supplies and materials in favour of such leagues/associations; otherwise, the Audit Team will be forced to issue the corresponding Notice of Disallowance (ND) for unauthorized use of government funds for such purposes.

We recommended that the management: (a) develop/execute policies and standards, subject to concurrence of the Sanggunian, relative to the granting of financial assistance and donations pursuant to Sections 36 and 447(5) of R.A. No. 7160 which shall include but not limited to the: (i) results of case studies; (ii) certificate of eligibility of recipients, and recommendations of various offices from the health service, social service, and the DILG Office, whichever is applicable; (iii) types/kinds of financial assistance; (iv) criteria on eligibility; (v) threshold/limits of entitlement of required documents; and (vi) manner of monitoring the actual expenditures by the recipients. The implementing guidelines will govern the assessment, evaluation, grant and release of financial assistance; thus, the exercise of judicious and prudent judgement on each request received is ensured; and (b) strengthen the internal control system of the Agency by (i) implementing all existing laws, rules and regulations relevant to the disbursements; and (ii) instructing the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review of the financial transactions prior to payment thereof in order to ensure that expenditures are valid, legal and necessary to the attainment of program goals and objectives.

We recommended that:
all certifying and approving officials in the disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents strictly adhere to the fundamental principles in the disbursement of funds prescribed under Section 4, Paragraphs 5 and 6 of P.D. No. 1445; and
the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of minimum documentary requirements for all types of the transactions and process only claims that are properly signed and certified  by the authorized officials. Otherwise, Notice of Suspension (NS) and/or Notice of Disallowance (ND) shall be issued on such deficient claims.

We recommended that the: (a) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the timely rendition of accounts and financial reports by observing the prescribed due dates so as not to delay the verification of the transactions and post-audit activities; and (b) Local Chief Executive impose sanctions against violators to ensure timely and relevant reporting to the COA, stakeholders and decision-making bodies.

We recommended that the: (a) Members of the Sangguniang Bayan consider (i) enacting appropriate ordinance on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyances to prevent undesirable outcomes as a result of unregulated proliferation of tricycles along the national highways, which may pose hazards and imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties; and (ii) impose sanctions against illegal parking; and (b) Local Chief Executive instruct the traffic enforcers to diligently perform their duties by strictly monitoring the condition of the parking areas within the right-of-way of national roads, particularly along the central business district of  the Municipality to comply with the DPWH Department Order No. 73, and deploy more traffic enforcers during market days and busy hours. (ii) continue the use of alternate routes for tricycles and should be strictly imposed by the traffic enforcers since it already gained positive impact on commuters through faster travel time and had minimized the inconvenience of transporting from one place to another.

We recommended that the: (a) management capacitate all the Municipal Officials and the GAD Committee/Focal Point on gender mainstreaming, gender-responsive planning, budgeting, gender analysis and gender assessment, and development and utilization of GAD tools;  (b) GFPS (i) maintain a GAD Database containing not only a master list of senior citizens, solo parents and day-care beneficiaries but also GAD related information, such as surveys, statistics, studies and various reports to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning; (ii) administer the HGDG tests to aid them in the formulation of GAD PPAs that specifically address the gender gaps/issues and concerns and work towards the attainment of their targeted outputs; and (iii) formulate, pass and implement Municipal GAD Codes or ordinances to support the Municipality’s efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s rights, women’s economic empowerment, and gender-responsive governance towards the attainment of gender equality and women’s empowerment; (c) GAD Focal person furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the annual GAD accomplishment report submitted to the DILG and PCW, accompanied by the following: (i) brief summary of the reported program or project; (ii) copies of reported policy issuances; (iii) results of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) test; and  (iv) actions taken by the Municipality on the COA audit findings and recommendations;  and (d) Municipal Mayor issue an appropriate policy, directive, copy furnished the DILG Regional Office to disseminate and implement the Municipal GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) upon receipt of the enacted GPB and certificate of approval from the DILG Regional Office.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal  Environment and Management Officer to: (i) intensify awareness of  the constituents, thru creative information campaign and education activities  in schools, barangays, commercial establishments and  private or government offices, on the “no segregation, no collection policy” and the “no-littering policy” of the Municipality to reduce service costs incurred in bringing garbage from the source to the MRFs/Landfill; (ii) make representations with the Sanguniang Pambayan to consider the passage of legislations strengthening such waste management policies;(iii) involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation of existing environmental laws, especially in striving to operationalize existing Materials Recovery Facility (MRFs) and/or establish new ones in their respective barangays; (iv) strictly enforce the sanctions to violators of the rules and regulations governing the ecological solid waste management; and exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9003.

We recommended that the Members of the Local School Board (i) refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities in which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; (ii) request for inputs from the DepEd official/School Division Superintendent in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures in accordance with the DepEd criteria and with due regard to the provisions of laws and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the fund; (iii) maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its objectives.; and (iv) submit legal basis in the giving of Local School Board Allowance and Teachers’ Allowance, otherwise, the grant will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the: (a) implementing departments of the Municipality (i) to furnish the Office of the Auditor with Project Proposals, MOA, SIA, and documents to support changes made in the implementation of BuB projects/programs (ii) to submit immediately to the Accounting Unit all the necessary documents for the immediate submission of liquidation reports to the National Government Agencies concerned so that the succeeding release of BuB funds will not be delayed; (b) Accounting unit to ensure submission of complete documentary requirements before payment of disbursements and immediately submit all the lacking documents to the Office of the Auditor; otherwise a Notice of Suspension (NS) will be issued; and (c)  Local Poverty Reduction and Action Team (LPRAT) to closely monitor the  projects that were identified and programmed under the Bottom-Up Budgeting to facilitate the implementation thereof for the sake of public interest.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the personnel in charge to (i) refrain from using the DRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not directly related to disaster preparedness, mitigation, response and rescue operations; and (ii) ensure that the 30% QRF would only be released during the declaration of state of calamity and utilized only for relief and recovery programs; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to (i) transfer the remaining balance of LDRRMF-MOOE to Trust Fund; (ii) prepare and submit a certified monthly Report on the Utilization of DRRMF; (iii) submit all the disbursement vouchers after the end of each month for timely verification; and (iv) maintain RAAO in the required format for proper monitoring; and (c) ensure that the LDRRMC is composed of the prescribed number of members and that they carry out the duties and responsibilities attached to their mandated functions pursuant to R.A. No. 10121.

We recommended that the management: (a) instruct Department heads/personnel to take full responsibility of the preparation of the required reports and strictly observe the timely submission of the same to the Monitoring Committee; (b) advice the Monitoring Committee to comply with the full disclosure policy requirements specifically on the prescribed period and modalities  of posting the required financial documents in at least three conspicuous places of the Municipality and in the Full Disclosure Policy Portal pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2013-140; and (c) direct the General Services Officer and Municipal Engineer to strictly comply with the required posting of information and publicity on programs/projects/activities of government agencies and inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the projects or before the start of the programs/projects/activities that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the project sites.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and members of the MDC (a) prepare and furnish the Office of the Auditor with the Report on Utilization or accomplishment reports on the 20% Development Fund for proper verification and to be able to render more appropriate audit disposition on the use of the said Fund; (b) present the PPAs in the AIP of the 20% Development Fund by sectoral type to properly identify the socio-economic and environmental projects proposed; (c) unburden the 20% Development Fund with charges for debt-servicing by appropriating a reasonable amount against the total regular income of the LGU after deduction of all statutory and contractual obligations of the LGU as provided under Section 324 (b) of the Local Government Code; (c) give priority on high-impact capital expenditures that provide long-term development benefits to constituents in accordance with the DBM-DILG JMC 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011; and (d) implement the PPAs in accordance with the plans and targets for the year.
We recommended that the Municipal  Government continually maintain the good practice of adhering to the provisions of R.A. No. 8291 on the withholding and remittance of GSIS premiums.
We recommended that the Municipal Government continually maintain the good practice of adhering to the provisions of R.A. No. 8291 on the withholding and remittance of GSIS premiums.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the persons liable to settle immediately the audit disallowances totaling ₱421,889.94 pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; otherwise, as the agency head, initiate legal/administrative sanctions for their continued failure to make settlement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) OIC-Market Supervisor to strictly enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the Contract of Lease relative to the period and mode of payment for rental of market stalls; imposition of surcharge/s and interest for late and non-payment of rentals; and enforcement of the provision for possible cancellation of the contracts in case of delinquency; (b) the Municipal Treasurer send demand letters to the delinquent stallholders in order to avoid possible distraint of goods, chattels, or effects, and other personal property; and (c) the Municipal Accountant to record the rental receivable from the unpaid rental stalls amounting to ₱3,006,891.50.

observations.managementAction:
The relief from custodial accountability for the lost documents was filed to the COA Audit Team Leader on February 29, 2016.

An investigation  committee was created on April 4, 2017  to investigate the facts and circumstances surrounding the loss of the documents.
Management stopped making payments through reimbursement basis.
Management already created an Inventory Committee and a Physical Inventory Report was submitted.

Management installed the GSO as the inspector of the deliveries while the end-user /department head was tasked to take charge of the acceptance of deliveries.

The GSO started maintaining stock cards on the latter part of CY 2016.

Management provided a bodega near PESO Office for storage of items procured.
